{{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}, {{transl|ja|せっかん}}, {{transl|ja|せきかん}}, refers to a coffin made from [[stone material]]. This article provides an overview of stone coffins from around the world. [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[ja:石棺]] == Stone Coffins in Japan == This section explains the stone coffins in ancient Japan. [[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. === Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period === Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ === Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period === * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. ; Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. === References === {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. === Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period === Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ === Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period === * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. ; Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. === References === {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. === Related topics === * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[ja:石棺]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] ==References==

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. === Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period === Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ === Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period === * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. ; Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. === References === {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. === Related topics === * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[category:Sekkan]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sekkan]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] ==References==

In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.

Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[category:most recent 500]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.

Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.

Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. ==References==

{{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "|陶棺}}" to "|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}"
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "|割竹形石棺}}" to "|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}"
revision.text:

Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]]
