{{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:石棺]] '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|12=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|12=draft}}.

Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|12=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|12=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|12=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|12=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|12=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] {{draft categories| [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:石棺]] [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:日本の石棺]]

Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}}, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|12=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|12=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|12=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|12=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|12=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|12=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|12=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|12=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|12=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|12=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:石棺]] [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]]

Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|12=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|12=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|12=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}}, eds. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|12=draft}}. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|12=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|12=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|12=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|12=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|12=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. {{Otheruses|Stone coffins worldwide|Concrete structure for containing the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant reactor|Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant disaster}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|12=draft|12=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft|12=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|12=draft|12=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft|12=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft|12=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft|12=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft|12=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft|12=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft|12=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|12=draft|12=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft|12=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|12=draft|12=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft|12=draft}}, eds. Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.

Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]]

'''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]]Subst for Coffins to comment

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
