Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|箱式石棺" to "|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin|"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin||qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]]

By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin||qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]]

revision.comment: 35 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:石棺 up to rev 100400894
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|世界の石造りの棺|チェルノブイリ原子力発電所の原子炉封じ込めのためのコンクリート構造物|チェルノブイリ原子力発電所事故}} '''石棺'''（せっかん、せきかん、{{Lang-en-short|[[:en:stone coffin|stone coffin]]あるいは[[:en:sarcophagus|sarcophagus]]}}）は、[[石材]]で造られた[[棺]]。 当記事では世界各地の石棺について解説する。 == 概説 == 世界各地で、さまざまな時代で、石棺は用いられている。 たとえば[[古代エジプト]]、[[古代ギリシア]]、[[古代ローマ]]（[[ローマ帝国]]）、[[中世]][[ヨーロッパ]]で用いられている。 古代エジプトの[[エジプト新王国]]時代には[[ファラオ]]が石棺に入れて埋葬されていたことが知られている。 「[[アレキサンダー大王]]の石棺」は、現在はトルコの[[イスタンブール考古学博物館]]に（実物が）展示されている[https://www.travel-zentech.jp/gaiphoto/turky/istanbul_95_024_m.htm イスタンブール/考古学博物館の写真 - 旅行のとも、ZenTech]。 現代でも[[バチカン]]では[[教皇]]が亡くなればその遺体は木製の棺に入れた上で石棺に納める[https://www.cbcj.catholic.jp/faq/hitsugi/ 教皇の遺体を納める棺とは？ | カトリック中央協議会]。 なお古代エジプト、古代ギリシア、古代ローマなどの石棺が[[サルコファガス]]と呼ばれている[https://www.lexico.com/definition/sarcophagus]。（つまり「古代エジプトのもの(だけ)をサルコファガスと呼ぶ」という主張は誤りである。） [[中東]]の地にあった古代[[遺跡]]からは多くの石棺が発掘されてきた歴史がある。たとえば[[バビロニア]]の王たちの墓からは石棺が出土している。 [[フェニキア]]の地からは、紀元前850年ごろのものと推定されている[[アヒラム王の石棺]]も見つかっている。 また東アジアでは[[カンボジア]]、[[中国]]、[[朝鮮半島]]、[[日本]]などの権力者によって石棺は用いられた。古代の中国・朝鮮半島・日本はいわばひとつの文化圏であり、それらの石棺には影響関係が認められる。 アメリカ大陸では[[メソアメリカ]]文化で石棺が用いられた。[[ペルー]]の[[カラヒア遺跡]]からは、人型石棺（[[:en:Sarcophagi_of_Carajía]]）が見つかった。マヤ文明の[[パレンケ]]の地下遺跡でも石棺が発見されている。 {{Gallery |width = 250px |File:Ahiram_sarcophag_from_Biblos_XIII-XBC.jpg|[[アヒラム王の石棺]] |Stone coffin unearthed in Allerton Park, Yorkshire (O57778).jpg|イギリス、ノース・ヨークシャーの土中から掘り出された石棺（1901年） |Stone coffin - geograph.org.uk - 633277.jpg| All Saints' churchの[[教会堂]]の外に（無造作に）放置されている[[中世]]の石棺。 |Liao Shangjing Museum 2017 stone coffins B.jpg|[[晋 (王朝)]]時代の石棺 |Northern Wei Stone Coffin.jpg|[[北魏]]の石棺（河南博物館展示品）。 }} もともとは彫刻などで装飾をほどこされたものを特にsarcophagusサルコファガスと呼んでいたが、彫刻をほどこしていない質素な石棺も「sarcophagusサルコファガス」と呼ばれることが増えている。 == 日本の石棺 == 本節内では、古代日本における石棺について説明する。 [[ファイル:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|[[前塚古墳 (高槻市)|前塚古墳]]出土 [[長持形石棺]]}}{{small|[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]]展示。}}]] 棺には、石棺の他に[[壺棺]]・[[甕棺]]・[[木棺]]・[[陶棺]]・[[乾漆棺]]などがある。
そのうち石棺は最も堅牢で密閉性に優れ、遺骸の保存に適している。 [[縄文時代|縄文]]・[[弥生時代|弥生]]・[[古墳時代|古墳]]の各時代に造られ使用された。 石棺には、[[天然]]の扁平な石を組み合わせただけのやや小型の箱式石棺と、石材を加工した大型の石棺とに分類することができる。
箱式石棺は大陸でも見られ、[[縄文時代]]後期に東日本で広がったものと[[弥生時代]]初期に[[稲作]]とともに北九州に渡来したものがある。
大型の石棺は弥生時代にも散見されるものの、主には[[古墳時代]]の特色とされている。
大型の石棺には、石材を刳り抜いたものと組み合わせたものがある。刳抜式の[[割竹形石棺]]・[[舟形石棺]]が[[4世紀]]後半に現れた。
[[5世紀]]には組合式の[[長持形石棺]]が近畿中央部を中心に広がり、中部九州や[[山陰地方|山陰]]・[[北陸地方|北陸]]・[[関東地方|関東]]などでは舟形石棺が発達した。
[[6世紀]]には、[[近畿]]・[[九州]]・[[山陽地方|山陽]]・[[山陰地方|山陰]]・[[東海地方|東海]]・[[関東地方|関東]]の一部に、地方の特色を備えた独自の刳抜式や組合式の[[家形石棺]]が盛んに用いられ、[[7世紀]]に入っても一部で継続して使用された。 '''縄文時代の石棺''' [[秋田県]][[北秋田郡]][[田代町 (秋田県)|田代町]]本郷矢石館遺跡で5基発見されている。石棺を包含する[[土層 (考古学)|土層]]と周辺から出土した[[土器]]から、これらは晩期のもと推定されている。[[青森県]]久原山野峠遺跡では6基発見されている。斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ '''弥生時代の石棺''' *'''[[箱式石棺]]'''が発達した。 ;古墳時代の石棺 *'''[[割竹形石棺]]'''（わりだけがたせっかん）[[割竹形木棺]]を真似て造られた石棺。 *'''[[舟形石棺]]'''（ふながたせっかん）蓋・身ともに刳り抜き式で、割竹形石棺の変容形とも解されている。 *'''[[長持形石棺]]'''（ながもちがたせっかん）長さ2 - 3メートルで、身は箱形の組合式。 *'''[[家形石棺]]'''（いえがたせっかん）身は箱形で、蓋は屋根形をしている。 '''日本の節の参考文献''' {{脚注の不足|section=1|date=2016年3月}} *[[江坂輝彌]]・[[芹沢長介]]・[[坂詰秀一]]編『新日本考古学小辞典』[[ニュー・サイエンス社]] 2005年 ISBN 4-8216-0511-2 *[[斎藤忠]]『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』[[学生社]] 2004年 ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5 *[[田中琢]]・[[佐原真]]編『日本考古学事典』[[三省堂]] 2003年 ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8 *[[永原慶二]]監修 [[石上英一]]他編集『岩波 日本史辞典』[[岩波書店]] 1999年 ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8 ===日本の節の関連項目=== *[[古墳]] *[[弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|石棺墓]] *[[弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|木棺墓]] *[[石棺式石室]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せつかん}} [[Category:棺]] [[Category:石]] [[en:Sarcophagus]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[石棺]] to [[Sekkan]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:

そのうち石棺は最も堅牢で密閉性に優れ、遺骸の保存に適している。 [[縄文時代|縄文]]・[[弥生時代|弥生]]・[[古墳時代|古墳]]の各時代に造られ使用された。 石棺には、[[天然]]の扁平な石を組み合わせただけのやや小型の箱式石棺と、石材を加工した大型の石棺とに分類することができる。
箱式石棺は大陸でも見られ、[[縄文時代]]後期に東日本で広がったものと[[弥生時代]]初期に[[稲作]]とともに北九州に渡来したものがある。

大型の石棺には、石材を刳り抜いたものと組み合わせたものがある。刳抜式の[[割竹形石棺]]・[[舟形石棺]]が[[4世紀]]後半に現れた。
[[5世紀]]には組合式の[[長持形石棺]]が近畿中央部を中心に広がり、中部九州や[[山陰地方|山陰]]・[[北陸地方|北陸]]・[[関東地方|関東]]などでは舟形石棺が発達した。
[[6世紀]]には、[[近畿]]・[[九州]]・[[山陽地方|山陽]]・[[山陰地方|山陰]]・[[東海地方|東海]]・[[関東地方|関東]]の一部に、地方の特色を備えた独自の刳抜式や組合式の[[家形石棺]]が盛んに用いられ、[[7世紀]]に入っても一部で継続して使用された。 '''縄文時代の石棺''' [[秋田県]][[北秋田郡]][[田代町 (秋田県)|田代町]]本郷矢石館遺跡で5基発見されている。石棺を包含する[[土層 (考古学)|土層]]と周辺から出土した[[土器]]から、これらは晩期のもと推定されている。[[青森県]]久原山野峠遺跡では6基発見されている。斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004年 248ページ '''弥生時代の石棺''' *'''[[箱式石棺]]'''が発達した。 ;古墳時代の石棺 *'''[[割竹形石棺]]'''（わりだけがたせっかん）[[割竹形木棺]]を真似て造られた石棺。 *'''[[舟形石棺]]'''（ふながたせっかん）蓋・身ともに刳り抜き式で、割竹形石棺の変容形とも解されている。 *'''[[長持形石棺]]'''（ながもちがたせっかん）長さ2 - 3メートルで、身は箱形の組合式。 *'''[[家形石棺]]'''（いえがたせっかん）身は箱形で、蓋は屋根形をしている。 '''日本の節の参考文献''' {{脚注の不足|section=1|date=2016年3月}} *[[江坂輝彌]]・[[芹沢長介]]・[[坂詰秀一]]編『新日本考古学小辞典』[[ニュー・サイエンス社]] 2005年 ISBN 4-8216-0511-2 *[[斎藤忠]]『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』[[学生社]] 2004年 ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5 *[[田中琢]]・[[佐原真]]編『日本考古学事典』[[三省堂]] 2003年 ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8 *[[永原慶二]]監修 [[石上英一]]他編集『岩波 日本史辞典』[[岩波書店]] 1999年 ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8 ===日本の節の関連項目=== *[[古墳]] *[[弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|石棺墓]] *[[弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|木棺墓]] *[[石棺式石室]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せつかん}} [[Category:棺]] [[Category:石]] [[en:Sarcophagus]]

{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin||qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == Related topics == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Sekkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.

Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin||qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]]

{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin||qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, eds. Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōmon period|qq=}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'in Region|qq=}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokuriku Region|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō Region|qq=}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki|qq=}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu|qq=}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yō Region|qq=}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'in Region|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Region|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō Region|qq=}}. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Prefecture|qq=}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeological terms]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Archaeological terms]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maetsuka Kofun|qq=}}}}{{small|On display at the {{ill|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|lt=Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|qq=}}.}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|qq=}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lacquered Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshitsu coffin|qq=}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōmon period|qq=}}, {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōmon period|qq=}} and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}} in the early {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}}.
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}}, are primarily considered a characteristic of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}.
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'in Region|qq=}}, {{ill|Hokuriku Region|ja|北陸地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokuriku Region|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō Region|qq=}}.
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki|qq=}}, {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu|qq=}}, {{ill|San'yō Region|ja|山陽地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yō Region|qq=}}, {{ill|San'in Region|ja|山陰地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'in Region|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōkai Region|ja|東海地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Region|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō Region|qq=}}. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in {{ill|Tashiro, Akita|ja|田代町 (秋田県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tashiro, Akita|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitaakita District, Akita|ja|北秋田郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaakita District, Akita|qq=}}. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soil Layer|qq=}} containing the coffins and nearby {{ill|Pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pottery|qq=}}, they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Prefecture|qq=}}.斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terumi Esaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsuke Serizawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūichi Sakazume|qq=}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Japanese stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]]

{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maetsuka Kofun|qq=}}}}{{small|On display at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|qq=}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lacquered Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshitsu coffin|qq=}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}} in the early [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]].
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], are primarily considered a characteristic of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]].
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku Region]], and [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki|qq=}}, [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]], [[:en:San'yō region|San'yō Region]], [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai Region]], and [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]]. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in [[:en:Tashiro, Akita|Tashiro, Akita]], [[:en:Kitaakita District, Akita|Kitaakita District, Akita]]. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soil Layer|qq=}} containing the coffins and nearby [[:en:Earthenware|Pottery]], they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terumi Esaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsuke Serizawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūichi Sakazume|qq=}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]]

Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terumi Esaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsuke Serizawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūichi Sakazume|qq=}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Stone (material)]]

{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maetsuka Kofun|qq=}}}}{{small|On display at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|qq=}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lacquered Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshitsu coffin|qq=}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}} in the early [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]].
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], are primarily considered a characteristic of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]].
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku Region]], and [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki|qq=}}, [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]], [[:en:San'yō region|San'yō Region]], [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai Region]], and [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]]. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in [[:en:Tashiro, Akita|Tashiro, Akita]], [[:en:Kitaakita District, Akita|Kitaakita District, Akita]]. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soil Layer|qq=}} containing the coffins and nearby [[:en:Earthenware|Pottery]], they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terumi Esaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsuke Serizawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūichi Sakazume|qq=}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese burials]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Stone (material)]] {{Commons category|Stone coffins}}

{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} '''Stone Coffin''' ({{lang-ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=}}) refers to a coffin made from stone material.[[File:前塚古墳出土 長持形石棺.JPG|280px|thumb|{{center|Stone coffin excavated from {{ill|Maetsuka Kofun|ja|前塚古墳 (高槻市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maetsuka Kofun|qq=}}}}{{small|On display at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].}}]] Besides stone coffins, there are also [[:en:Jar_burial|Jar Coffins]], {{ill|Clay Coffins|ja|甕棺|1=Clay coffin chamber|2=ja|3=粘土槨|lt=Clay Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, {{ill|Wooden Coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}, {{ill|Ceramic Coffins|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceramic Coffins|qq=}}, and {{ill|Lacquered Coffins|ja|乾漆棺|1=Kanshitsu coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lacquered Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshitsu coffin|qq=}}.
Among them, stone coffins are the most durable and airtight, making them suitable for preserving remains. They were crafted and used during the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Stone coffins can be classified into smaller box-shaped coffins made by assembling natural flat stones and larger coffins crafted from stone materials.
Box-shaped coffins were also seen on the continent, spreading to eastern Japan in the later [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] and arriving in northern Kyushu with {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}} in the early [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]].
Large stone coffins, though sporadically seen in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], are primarily considered a characteristic of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]].
Among large stone coffins, there are hollowed-out coffins and assembled ones. Hollowed {{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}} and {{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}} appeared in the late 4th century.
In the 5th century, {{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}} spread mainly in the central Kinki region, while boat-shaped coffins developed in central Kyushu, [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku Region]], and [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].
By the 6th century, unique local hollowed-out and assembled {{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}} were actively used in parts of {{ill|Kinki|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近畿地方}}, [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]], [[:en:San'yō region|San'yō Region]], [[:en:San'in region|San'in Region]], [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai Region]], and [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]]. Their use continued into the 7th century in some areas. == Stone Coffins in the Jōmon Period == Five stone coffins have been discovered at the Hon-go Yaiseki Ruins in [[:en:Tashiro, Akita|Tashiro, Akita]], [[:en:Kitaakita District, Akita|Kitaakita District, Akita]]. Based on the {{ill|Soil Layer|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soil Layer|qq=}} containing the coffins and nearby [[:en:Earthenware|Pottery]], they are estimated to belong to the late Jōmon period. Six coffins have been found at the Kuharayama-toge Ruins in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].斎藤忠『日本考古学用語辞典 改訂新版』学生社 2004th year 248ページ == Stone Coffins in the Yayoi Period == * '''{{ill|Box-shaped Coffins|ja|箱式石棺|1=Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Box-shaped stone coffin|qq=}}''' were developed. == Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terumi Esaka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsuke Serizawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūichi Sakazume|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}}, eds. "Shin Nihon Kōkogaku Kōjiten" (New Japanese Archaeological Dictionary). New Science Press, 2005. ISBN 4-8216-0511-2. * Saitō Tadashi. "Nihon Kōkogaku Yōgo Jiten Kaitei Shinpan" (Dictionary of Japanese Archaeological Terms, Revised Edition). Gakuseisha, 2004. ISBN 978-4-311-75033-5. * Tanaka Taku and Sahara Makoto, eds. "Nihon Kōkogaku Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology). Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8. * Keiji Nagahara (Supervisor), Eiichi Ishigami, et al. "Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten" (Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary). Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 978-4-00-080093-8. * ==References== {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|石棺|version=100400894|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Stone coffins]] [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:sekkan]] [[Category:Sekkan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] }} [[sn:Sekkan]] [[ja:石棺]] [[ja:日本の石棺]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Stone (material)]] {{Commons category|Stone coffins}}

Stone Coffins in the Kofun Period == * '''{{ill|Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|ja|割竹形石棺|1=Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split-bamboo-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (わりだけがたせっかん): Stone coffins modeled after split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffins. * '''{{ill|Boat-shaped Coffins|ja|舟形石棺|1=Funagata Sekkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boat-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Sekkan|qq=}}''' (ふながたせっかん): Both the lid and body were hollowed out, considered a variation of split-bamboo-shaped coffins. * '''{{ill|Long-box-shaped Coffins|ja|長持形石棺|1=Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-box-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (ながもちがたせっかん): Measuring 2–3 meters in length, the body was a box-shaped assembly. * '''{{ill|House-shaped Coffins|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}}''' (いえがたせっかん): The body was box-shaped, and the lid resembled a roof. == See Also == * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#石棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制#木棺墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin Tombs in the Yayoi Period|qq=}} * {{ill|Stone Coffin Chambers|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|qq=式石室|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Coffin Chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} == References == {{Unreferenced section|section=1|date=2016-03}} * {{ill|Terumi Esaka|ja|江坂輝彌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terumi Esaka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Chōsuke Serizawa|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsuke Serizawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shūichi Sakazume|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūichi Sakazume|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}}, eds.
